mappings.rationaleAI: Psychology as the scientific study of mind and behavior addresses the problem of defining attention (A.1), the development of theories of attention through stimulus-response and behaviourist studies (A.2), and contemporary theories of perception including the influence of Gestalt and behaviourist theories (C.1), providing the disciplinary framework for all perception and sensation research.

The ear's three anatomical regions — outer, middle (tympanic membrane and ossicles), and inner ear (cochlea) — cover the mechanisms of the external, middle, and inner ear and functions involved in sound transmission and conversion to neural messages (B.3.a). The semicircular canals, utricle, and saccule for balance cover vestibular senses and maintaining equilibrium (B.3.d, B.6).

Senses as biological systems for sensation via specialized receptor cells — mechanoreceptors, photoreceptors, chemoreceptors, thermoreceptors — cover the senses in general (B.1). Proprioception connects to kinesthesis and kinesthetic feedback in movement control (B.5), vestibular sense to maintaining equilibrium (B.6), and the breadth of sensory modalities to interrelations among the senses (E.3).

Consciousness as awareness of internal states or external objects — encompassing wakefulness, self-awareness, and metacognition — directly relates to attention as awareness of internal and external events (A) and the problem of defining attention (A.1).

Color perception arising from cone cell activation in the retina covers the work of the retina in vision (B.2.a), and perceptual phenomena such as trichromacy, metamerism, hue, saturation, and lightness cover perceptual processes of vision (B.2.c).

The eye as the sensory organ for photoreception covers the work of the retina with rod and cone photoreceptors (B.2.a) and the transduction process converting the retinal image into neural signals via electrochemical processes (B.2.b).

Sound as a mechanical wave phenomenon characterized by frequency, amplitude, and speed, bridging the physical wave and psychoacoustic perception of pitch, loudness, timbre, and spatial location, covers audition and the encoding, processing, and discrimination of auditory qualities (B.3.b).

The brain as the central organ for sensory integration and cognition covers physiological mechanisms of attention (A.5), and the cerebral cortex's role in processing visual and other sensory information relates to responses of the cerebral cortex to visual stimuli (B.2.d).

Mental disorders including psychotic conditions involve clinically significant disturbances in cognition that can produce hallucinations (G.2), with neurological factors in hallucinations (G.2.a) and types of hallucinatory experience across conditions (G.2.b).

Freud's theories of the unconscious, repression, and the structural model of the mind (id, ego, superego) address determinants of attention including unconscious influences, temperament, and interests (A.4).

Animation as a filmmaking technique creating the illusion of motion through sequential display of manipulated images directly relates to types of illusory experience (G.1) and visual cues for perceiving motion of objects (F.1), exploiting the phi phenomenon and persistence of vision.

Information theory (Shannon) — quantifying information in bits inversely proportional to event probability — directly relates to the analysis of attentive phenomena in terms of information theory (A.6).

Human behavior as the full range of mental, physical, and social responses shaped by psychological and cultural factors relates to attention as awareness of events (A), and individual differences in cognitive behavior and perception connect to differences in perceptual functioning among individuals (C.4).

Anesthesia is the pharmacological suppression of sensation and awareness, directly addressing awareness of events (A), the general functioning of the senses (B.1), and pain experience including its cause, qualities, and modes of treatment (B.4.d).
